Multiple Wingcopter patent filings 2017–2024
prior_art_notes: Wingcopter 198 is Germany's lead unmanned tilt-rotor cargo eVTOL — operational since 2018 (medical delivery to Pacific islands with UNICEF). Establishes prior art for: (1) small-airframe tilt-rotor cargo eVTOL with single-axis tilt mechanism (Wingcopter Tilt-Rotor Mechanism patent family), (2) the cargo / payload-delivery use case for tilt-rotor eVTOL distinct from passenger air taxi. Anticipates cargo eVTOL claims asserting novelty over single-axis tilt actuation.
propulsion_details: Four tilting electric prop-rotors at the corners of an X-frame; the Wingcopter Tilt-Rotor Mechanism (patented) uses a single tilt-axis actuator per rotor with mode-switching between hover, fast-cruise, and reverse-thrust orientations. Lithium-polymer battery pack.
sources: Wingcopter GmbH press releases 2017–2024.
Wingcopter Tilt-Rotor Mechanism patents EP3260370 / US10913529.
UNICEF Pacific islands medical delivery program reports 2018.

control_architecture: full autonomy with mission-planner ground station; Pyka-developed flight stack
creator: Pyka (Oakland, California)
disclosure_citation: Pyka founded 2018; Egret (predecessor agricultural eVTOL) operational since 2019; Pelican Cargo unveiled 2022-06-21; FAA Part 137 ag operations approval (Pelican Spray) 2023-04. Pyka's autonomy stack documented in open technical white papers.
ip_citations: Pyka patent estate, multiple US filings 2019–2024
prior_art_notes: Pyka Pelican is the leading US autonomous cargo eVTOL targeting agricultural and middle-mile logistics. Establishes prior art for: (1) autonomous lift+cruise cargo eVTOL operating under Part 137 / Part 23, (2) electric agricultural-spray eVTOL (Pelican Spray variant operational since 2023). Distinct architectural commitment from passenger eVTOL: prioritizes payload, range, and short-strip operation over hover endurance.
propulsion_details: Four electric lift propellers and one cruise propeller; autonomous fixed-wing eVTOL designed for short-strip / cargo delivery operations; lithium-ion battery.
sources: Pyka technical white papers 2020–2024.
FAA Part 137 / Special Conditions filings 2023.
Pyka press releases including 2022-06-21 Pelican unveil.

creator: Elroy Air Inc (San Francisco)
disclosure_citation: Elroy Air founded 2016 by David Merrill and Kofi Asante; sub-scale Chaparral demonstrator publicly unveiled 2019-01-30; full-scale C1 first hover flight 2023-11-12 at Camarillo, CA. Air Force AFWERX / USAF Agility Prime contracts 2021-09 onward.
ip_citations: Elroy Air patent estate, multiple US and PCT filings
prior_art_notes: Elroy Air Chaparral establishes prior art for: (1) hybrid-electric (turbine-genset + battery) cargo eVTOL, distinct from pure-battery designs by extending range to 500+ km, (2) modular swappable cargo-pod architecture decoupled from aircraft turnaround. Hybrid powerplant anticipates similar claims on series-hybrid eVTOL propulsion topology.
propulsion_details: Eight lift fans (four fore, four aft) plus four pusher propellers; turbine-electric hybrid genset (turbine-driven generator + battery + electric motor distribution). Modular cargo pod system swappable without aircraft contact.
sources: Elroy Air press releases and technical white papers 2019–2024.
USAF Agility Prime program disclosures.
FAA filings related to autonomous cargo operations.

creator: SZ DJI Technology Co Ltd (Shenzhen)
disclosure_citation: DJI FlyCart 30 publicly unveiled 2023-08-16 in Shenzhen; CAAC type certification application 2023; commercial deliveries to Chinese and international markets 2024. DJI is the world's largest drone manufacturer and holds an extensive multirotor patent estate.
ip_citations: DJI patent estate, vast (>1000 patents) covering multirotor architectures, autopilot, motors
prior_art_notes: DJI FlyCart 30 is the entry of the world's largest drone manufacturer into the heavy-cargo eVTOL category. DJI's massive patent estate and operational scale (millions of multirotor flights/day globally) constitute foundational prior-art coverage for: (1) coaxial-pair multirotor manufacturing at scale, (2) autonomous multirotor cargo delivery, (3) consumer-priced multirotor architecture migration to commercial cargo class. Establishes Chinese prior-art lineage for heavy-payload multirotor cargo eVTOL with explicit Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) operational basis.
propulsion_details: Four-axis eight-rotor coaxial-pair multicopter (four arms, two motors per arm); designed for 30 kg cargo capacity over 16 km range; lithium-ion battery.
sources: DJI press releases and product disclosures 2023–2024.
CAAC type certification dossier (DJI FlyCart 30).
DJI patent estate, USPTO and CNIPA records.

creator: IAT-21 / Innovative Aeronautical Technologies (Linz, Austria)
disclosure_citation: D-Dalus prototype publicly unveiled 2011-06-13 at Paris Air Show; co-developed with Sikorsky / United Technologies. Cycloidal rotor patents held by IAT-21 and earlier inventors (cf. Pateras Pescara's 1928 Italian and French cyclogiro patents — the cyclorotor concept originated with Pescara nearly a century earlier).
ip_citations: IAT-21 cycloidal rotor patents (Austrian and PCT)
Pescara cyclogiro patents (1928, expired) — foundational to the architecture
prior_art_notes: D-Dalus is the modern Austrian disclosure of cycloidal-rotor (cyclogiro) VTOL — a niche but distinct architecture that traces back to Pateras Pescara's 1928 cyclogiro patents. Establishes prior art for: (1) the cyclorotor lift architecture in modern technical literature, (2) omnidirectional thrust vectoring via azimuthal blade-pitch modulation. Pescara's foundational cyclogiro patents are long expired, placing the basic cyclorotor concept in the public domain. Anticipates modern exotic-VTOL claims (occasionally surfacing in cycloidal-fan or barrel-rotor patent filings) asserting novelty over the cyclorotor architecture.
propulsion_details: Four cycloidal rotors (cyclogiro) at the corners of a flat platform; each cyclogiro consists of a barrel rotor with parallel airfoil blades whose pitch is varied cyclically as a function of azimuth angle, directing thrust in any direction in the rotor plane. Combustion-engine driven.
sources: IAT-21 / Innovative Aeronautical Technologies disclosures 2011.
Sikorsky / United Technologies cyclorotor research publications.
Pescara, Raúl. Italian Patent on cyclogiro, 1928.
Hwang, Heng et al. 'Numerical Investigation of Cyclorotor Aerodynamics.' AIAA papers.

creator: Festo AG & Co. KG / Bionic Learning Network
disclosure_citation: Festo SmartBird publicly unveiled 2011-03-22 at TED Conference, Edinburgh; subsequently demonstrated at Hannover Messe 2011. Festo Bionic Learning Network technical white papers and design notes publicly released.
ip_citations: Festo SmartBird technical disclosures, no patent enforcement claimed
prior_art_notes: Festo SmartBird is the canonical modern engineering disclosure of controlled-flapping-wing flight. Establishes prior art for: (1) single-motor articulated-flapping-wing actuation, (2) active-twist wing control with embedded actuators, (3) lifelike biomimetic flapping-wing VTOL/STOL flight. Combined with laputa-flapter (1986 fictional), provides joint engineering+narrative prior-art base for any flapping-wing or ornithopter claim.
propulsion_details: Single brushless DC motor driving an articulated flapping-wing mechanism; carbon fiber and polyurethane foam structure; modeled on herring gull wing kinematics with active twist control.
sources: Festo Bionic Learning Network 'SmartBird' technical disclosure, 2011.
Send, R. and Festo team. 'SmartBird Aerodynamic Concept.' SAE technical paper, 2012.
Festo TED Talk and Hannover Messe demonstration materials.

control_architecture: rider lean-to-bank with electronic stability augmentation; differential rotor thrust for attitude
creator: Aerofex Corporation (Manhattan Beach, California)
disclosure_citation: Aerofex first concept demonstrator publicly unveiled October 2008; Aero-X full-scale prototype publicly demonstrated 2014-04-30. Founded by Mark De Roche, ex-Boeing/NASA; Aerofex hold patents on ducted-rotor stability augmentation.
ip_citations: US 8,083,173 (De Roche, 'Air vehicle and method for operation thereof')
prior_art_notes: Aerofex Aero-X is the canonical disclosure of the modern hover-bike architecture: rider-straddle platform with twin large lift rotors fore and aft. Establishes prior art for: (1) personal hover-bike form factor, (2) intuitive lean-to-bank stability control mapping rider body input to differential rotor thrust, (3) the open-rotor straddle motorcycle-like operational paradigm. Anticipates Hoversurf S3, JetPack Aviation Speeder, and any subsequent hover-bike patent attempt.
propulsion_details: Two large open-rotor lift fans fore and aft of the rider straddle platform; combustion engine drives both rotors via mechanical transmission. Aerofex's stability-augmentation control provides intuitive lean-to-bank rider input similar to a motorcycle.
sources: De Roche, Mark. US Patent 8,083,173, 2011.
Aerofex press releases and demonstration materials 2008–2017.
Popular Science and Wired coverage 2010–2014.

creator: Hoversurf Inc (Russia / United States)
disclosure_citation: Hoversurf Scorpion-3 prototype publicly unveiled 2017-04-28 in Moscow; first paying customer (Dubai Police) publicly disclosed 2017-10-13; FAA Part 103 Special Light-Sport Aircraft compliance evaluation 2018. Hoversurf founded 2014 by Alexander Atamanov.
ip_citations: Hoversurf Inc patent estate, Russian and US filings 2015–2024
prior_art_notes: Hoversurf Scorpion-3 is the leading commercial hover-bike — Russian-founded company with US operations, sold to Dubai Police as the first operational hover-bike patrol vehicle (2017). Establishes Russian prior-art lineage for the hover-bike form factor and confirms operational deployment pre-2018.
propulsion_details: Four large open-rotor lift propellers in an X-configuration around a rider-straddle motorcycle-style frame; lithium-ion battery; differential-thrust attitude control. Operates under FAA Part 103.
sources: Hoversurf press releases 2017–2024.
Dubai Police adoption announcement 2017-10-13.
FAA Part 103 ultralight registration records.

control_architecture: manual; pilot uses joysticks linked to fan cyclic pitch and engine throttle
creator: Martin Aircraft Company (Christchurch, New Zealand)
disclosure_citation: Martin Jetpack publicly unveiled 2008-07-29 at AirVenture Oshkosh, US; FAA approved as 'experimental category' aircraft 2010-08-22; first untethered piloted free-flight 2011-04-29. Glenn Martin holds NZ and US patents on the ducted-fan dual-rotor jetpack architecture.
ip_citations: NZ Patent 539537 (Martin, 2009)
US Patent 7,484,687 (Martin, 'Personal flight vehicle')
Multiple Martin Aircraft patents 2005–2018
prior_art_notes: Martin Jetpack is New Zealand's foundational disclosure of long-endurance personal-VTOL flight. Architecturally distinct from Bell Rocket Belt (rockets, 21 sec) and Gravity Industries (turbojets, 10 min) by using twin large ducted fans for ~30 min endurance. Establishes prior art for: (1) twin-ducted-fan personal-VTOL strap-on, (2) cyclic-pitch fan attitude control distinct from differential thrust, (3) FAA experimental-category personal jetpack certification basis. Glenn Martin's patents (NZ 539537, US 7,484,687) cover the architecture; some now expired.
propulsion_details: Two large ducted lift fans driven by a single 200 hp two-stroke V4 petrol engine; pilot strap-on; cyclic-pitch fans for attitude. Distinct from rocket-belt-class jetpacks by using ducted fans rather than turbojets/rockets — gives long endurance (~30 min).
sources: Martin, Glenn. NZ Patent 539537, 2009.
Martin, Glenn. US Patent 7,484,687, 2009.
Martin Aircraft Company press releases and SEC filings (NZX, 2015 IPO).

creator: ASKA / NFT Inc (Mountain View, California / Nagoya, Japan)
disclosure_citation: ASKA / NFT Inc founded 2018 by Guy Kaplinsky and Maki Kaplinsky; A5 production-design unveiled at CES 2023 (2023-01-04); FAA Special Airworthiness Certificate (Experimental) granted 2023-06-29 for flight testing. NHTSA-approved street-legal road operation pending.
ip_citations: NFT Inc / ASKA patent estate, multiple US filings 2018–2024
prior_art_notes: ASKA A5 is the leading drive+fly transformer eVTOL with documented FAA experimental-category certification. Establishes prior art for: (1) folding-wing tilt-rotor transformer architecture, (2) hybrid-electric powerplant for transformer range extension, (3) dual-certification basis (FAA + NHTSA road-legal). Anticipates: PAL-V Liberty, AeroMobil 4.0, Klein Vision AirCar, and any modern drive+fly patent claim.
propulsion_details: Six tilting electric prop-rotors mounted on folding wings that retract for road operation; serial-hybrid powerplant (gasoline genset + battery) for ~400 km flight range. NHTSA-compliant road wheels and lights.
sources: ASKA / NFT Inc press releases 2018–2024.
FAA Special Airworthiness Certificate dossier (ASKA A5).
ASKA technical white papers and patent filings.

creator: AMSL Aero Pty Ltd (Bankstown, Australia)
disclosure_citation: AMSL Aero Vertiia design publicly unveiled March 2021; sub-scale prototype hover testing 2022; first hover flight 2023-02-15. AMSL Aero founded 2017 by Andrew Moore (former CSIRO and Boeing engineer) and Siobhan Lyndon. Targeted at hydrogen-fuel-cell-powered eVTOL ambulance and regional connectivity missions.
ip_citations: AMSL Aero patent estate, Australian and PCT filings
prior_art_notes: AMSL Aero Vertiia is Australia's lead commercial eVTOL with a distinctive commitment to hydrogen-fuel-cell propulsion for 1,000 km range — addressing the energy-density limitation of pure-battery eVTOL. Establishes Australian prior-art lineage for hydrogen-eVTOL and for long-range regional eVTOL distinct from short-range urban air taxi. Anticipates other hydrogen-fuel-cell eVTOL claims (e.g., Joby's post-acquisition H2 program, Airbus ZEROe rotorcraft).
propulsion_details: Eight lift propellers and two pusher cruise propellers; hydrogen fuel cells provide electrical power (with backup battery) — distinct architectural commitment to hydrogen as primary energy source for range extension to 1,000 km.
sources: AMSL Aero press releases 2021–2024.
Australian Civil Aviation Safety Authority (CASA) eVTOL framework filings.
Vertical Flight Society Forum 2023 papers from AMSL.

creator: Pegasus Universal Aerospace (Johannesburg, South Africa)
disclosure_citation: Pegasus Vertical Business Jet design publicly unveiled 2018-11-08 at Aero South Africa; subsequent design refinements 2020–2024. Pegasus Universal Aerospace founded 2017 by Reza Mia. South African Civil Aviation Authority engagement on certification framework 2021 onward.
ip_citations: Pegasus Universal Aerospace patent estate, South African and PCT filings
prior_art_notes: Pegasus Vertical Business Jet is South Africa's lead commercial VTOL design — distinct architectural commitment to executive-jet operations (2,200 km range, 400 kt cruise) with VTOL hover capability. Establishes African continental prior-art lineage for commercial VTOL business aviation distinct from urban air taxi. Architecturally similar to Lockheed Martin / Aurora LightningStrike (XV-24A, US military 2016) in fuselage-internal lift fan + cruise jet topology.
propulsion_details: Twin internal lift fans embedded in fuselage for hover (vertical lift); twin cruise jet engines mounted on rear empennage for horizontal flight; integrated hybrid-electric architecture. Combines executive-jet form factor with VTOL capability.
sources: Pegasus Universal Aerospace press releases 2018–2024.
South African Civil Aviation Authority engagement records.
Aero South Africa exhibition materials 2018-11-08.

aliases: 小鹏汇天 陆地航母
creator: AeroHT (XPENG flying car subsidiary)
disclosure_citation: XPENG AeroHT Land Aircraft Carrier publicly unveiled 2024-04-04 at Beijing Auto Show; production target 2026. Combines a road-legal 6x6 ground vehicle ('Mothership') containing a fold-out manned eVTOL drone.
ip_citations: AeroHT / XPENG patent estate, multiple Chinese filings
prior_art_notes: XPENG AeroHT Land Aircraft Carrier is the most ambitious modular drive+fly transformer architecture publicly disclosed: a separate ground vehicle that carries and deploys a manned eVTOL. Establishes Chinese prior-art lineage for modular vehicle-eVTOL architectures, distinct from integrated transformer-eVTOL designs (ASKA A5, AeroMobil) where one vehicle physically transforms. Anticipates future modular-carrier patent filings.
propulsion_details: Modular two-component system: (1) road-legal 6x6 hybrid-electric ground vehicle that carries (2) a fold-out single-pilot multirotor eVTOL deployed from the rear of the vehicle. The eVTOL has six rotors in a deployable wing arrangement.
sources: XPENG AeroHT Beijing Auto Show 2024-04-04 unveiling materials.
AeroHT / XPENG technical disclosures 2024.
Reuters and South China Morning Post coverage 2024-04-04.

creator: Gerry and Sylvia Anderson / AP Films / ITC
disclosure_citation: Thunderbirds, episode 1 'Trapped in the Sky', original air date 1965-09-30 on ATV (UK). Thunderbird 2 designed by Derek Meddings and Reg Hill; design materials documented in Thunderbirds: Vehicles and Equipment (Marcus Hearn, 2015) and ITC production archives.
ip_citations: ITV Studios / Gerry Anderson Estate copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Thunderbird 2 establishes a 1965 cinematic disclosure of a heavy-lift VTOL cargo transport with modular swappable cargo pods — the fictional precursor to Elroy Air Chaparral's modular cargo-pod architecture (2019) by 54 years. Establishes British fictional prior art for: (1) heavy-lift VTOL cargo aircraft, (2) modular detachable cargo-pod system, (3) rescue/utility VTOL operational concept. Combined with bladerunner-spinner and jetsons-aerocar, provides 1962–1982 fictional VTOL anchor base.
propulsion_details: Heavy-lift VTOL transport with four ducted lift jets in the lower fuselage and four sustainer jet engines for cruise; capable of carrying modular detachable cargo pods ('Pods 1-6') containing rescue equipment. Wings extend during cruise mode.
sources: Thunderbirds, Gerry Anderson / AP Films / ITC, 1965 series.
Hearn, Marcus. Thunderbirds: Vehicles and Equipment. ITV Studios, 2015.
Bentley, Chris. The Complete Book of Thunderbirds. Carlton, 2008.

aliases: 風の谷のナウシカ Tolmekian gunship
canonical_name: Tolmekian Gunship (Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind)
creator: Hayao Miyazaki / Studio Topcraft (later Studio Ghibli)
disclosure_citation: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind (風の谷のナウシカ Kaze no Tani no Naushika), directed by Hayao Miyazaki, theatrical release 1984-03-11 in Japan. Tolmekian gunships and other aircraft documented in The Art of Nausicaä (Tokuma Shoten, 1984) and Miyazaki's earlier serialized manga (1982-1994).
ip_citations: Studio Ghibli / Tokuma Shoten copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Miyazaki's Tolmekian gunship establishes a Japanese cinematic disclosure of heavy military VTOL with multi-fan distributed lift architecture. The Art of Nausicaä (Tokuma Shoten, 1984) and the 1982-1994 manga provide detailed engineering depictions beyond the film. Establishes additional non-anglosphere fictional prior-art base for ducted-fan-array VTOL (with bladerunner-spinner and lilium-jet).
propulsion_details: Heavy military VTOL gunship with multiple ducted lift fans embedded in fuselage and wings; combination jet/propeller propulsion; armored troop-transport configuration. Distinctive industrial 1920s-aesthetic engine design with visible pistons and pipework.
sources: Miyazaki, Hayao (dir.). Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind / 風の谷のナウシカ. Studio Topcraft, 1984.
The Art of Nausicaä / 風の谷のナウシカ ジ・アート. Tokuma Shoten, 1984.
Miyazaki, Hayao. Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind manga. Animage / Tokuma Shoten, 1982–1994.

creator: Klein Vision s.r.o (Nitra, Slovakia)
disclosure_citation: Klein Vision AirCar Prototype 1 first flight test 2020-10; first inter-airport flight 2021-06-28 (Nitra to Bratislava). Slovak Transport Authority issued Certificate of Airworthiness 2022-01-26 — the world's first roadable-aircraft type certificate. Klein Vision founded by Stefan Klein.
ip_citations: Klein Vision patent estate, Slovak / EU / PCT filings 2018–2024
prior_art_notes: Klein Vision AirCar holds the world's first type-certified roadable-aircraft certificate (Slovak Transport Authority, 2022-01-26). Establishes Slovak prior-art lineage and certified-product anchor for the drive+fly transformer category. Not VTOL but architecturally adjacent — many drive+fly eVTOL designs (ASKA A5, AeroMobil 4.0) build on the same retractable-wing transformer architecture pioneered in road-legal aviation by Klein.
propulsion_details: Conventional fixed-wing aircraft with retractable wings that fold into rear fuselage; 1.6 L BMW engine drives both road wheels and rear pusher propeller; transitions from car to aircraft in 2 minutes 15 seconds via push-button mechanism. Not strictly VTOL — STOL — but establishes drive+fly transformer prior art.
sources: Klein Vision press releases 2020–2024.
Slovak Transport Authority Certificate of Airworthiness, 2022-01-26.
Klein, Stefan. AirCar technical white paper, 2021.

creator: AeroMobil s.r.o (Bratislava, Slovakia)
disclosure_citation: AeroMobil 4.0 publicly unveiled 2017-04-20 at Top Marques Monaco; predecessor AeroMobil 3.0 first flew 2014-10-29 (with crash 2015-05-08, pilot Stefan Klein survived; Klein subsequently founded Klein Vision). AeroMobil holds patents on retractable-wing roadable aircraft.
ip_citations: AeroMobil patent estate, Slovak / EU / PCT filings 2010–2024
prior_art_notes: AeroMobil 4.0 is Slovakia's other major drive+fly transformer disclosure (Klein departed AeroMobil after the 3.0 crash and founded Klein Vision). Establishes parallel Slovak prior-art lineage with hybrid-parallel powerplant — distinct from Klein Vision's pure-combustion approach. Combined with klein-vision-aircar, places drive+fly transformer prior art comprehensively in Slovak hands. Anticipates ASKA A5 and all subsequent drive+fly transformer claims.
propulsion_details: Hybrid parallel powerplant (combustion + electric) driving rear pusher propeller in flight and front road wheels in driving mode. Wings deploy rearward from fuselage in roughly 3 minutes.
sources: AeroMobil press releases and Top Marques Monaco 2017 unveiling.
AeroMobil patent estate, EUIPO and SUIPO records.
Reuters and Aviation Week coverage 2014–2018.

aliases: PAL-V Personal Air and Land Vehicle
creator: PAL-V International B.V. (Raamsdonksveer, Netherlands)
disclosure_citation: PAL-V One prototype first flight 2012-04-01; PAL-V Liberty production design unveiled 2017-03-07 at Geneva Motor Show; received EASA Type Certificate Part 27 (CS-27) for autogyro mode 2024-04-29. PAL-V founded 2008 by John Bakker.
ip_citations: PAL-V patent estate, Dutch / EU / PCT filings 2008–2024
prior_art_notes: PAL-V Liberty is the Netherlands' lead drive+fly transformer — architecturally distinct from Slovak peers (Klein/AeroMobil) by using autogyro lift rather than fixed-wing flight. Holds EASA Type Certificate Part 27 (autogyro mode), 2024-04-29 — the world's first type-certified flying car under autogyro classification. Direct architectural descendant of Cierva autogyro (1923) in road-legal passenger form factor.
propulsion_details: Two Rotax 912iS gasoline engines (one for road drive, one for flight); two-blade autorotating main rotor for lift in autogyro mode; pusher propeller aft for forward thrust; rotor folds for road operation. Cierva-style autogyro lineage in road-legal form factor.
sources: PAL-V press releases 2008–2024.
EASA Type Certificate Part 27, 2024-04-29 (PAL-V Liberty).
PAL-V technical white papers and patent filings.

creator: Sikorsky Aircraft (Lockheed Martin) / Boeing
disclosure_citation: Sikorsky-Boeing SB>1 Defiant first flight 2019-03-21 at West Palm Beach FL (U.S. Army Future Vertical Lift / Joint Multi-Role Technology Demonstrator program). Predecessor Sikorsky X2 first flight 2008-08-27. Sikorsky holds extensive coaxial-rigid-rotor patent estate.
ip_citations: Sikorsky X2 / Defiant patent estate, multiple US filings 2005–2024
U.S. Patents on rigid coaxial rotor technology
prior_art_notes: Sikorsky-Boeing Defiant establishes prior art for: (1) modern rigid coaxial counter-rotating rotor design overcoming retreating-blade-stall speed limits, (2) compound rotorcraft architecture (lift rotor + auxiliary cruise propulsion). Although not eVTOL, the architectural patterns directly anticipate any modern compound-rotorcraft eVTOL (Jaunt Journey, Karem AR40) claiming coaxial-plus-pusher novelty.
propulsion_details: Two coaxial counter-rotating rigid main rotors (descended from Sikorsky's X2 technology) for primary lift; rear pusher propeller for forward thrust. Rigid rotor design enables Advanced Blade-Tip Mach Number (ABMC) operation past conventional helicopter speed limits.
sources: Sikorsky / Boeing JMR-TD program disclosures.
Sikorsky X2 technology white papers, AHS / VFS Forums 2008–2024.
U.S. Army Future Vertical Lift program reports.

creator: Karem Aircraft Inc (Lake Forest, California)
disclosure_citation: Karem Aircraft AR40 / Optimum Speed Tilt-Rotor concept publicly disclosed in U.S. Army FVL program documentation 2008-2010; AR40 design materials in DARPA / Army Heavy Lift program filings. Karem holds Optimum Speed Rotor patents covering variable-RPM rotor operation.
ip_citations: Karem Aircraft Optimum Speed Rotor patents, multiple US filings
US Patents on slowed-rotor technology
prior_art_notes: Karem AR40 establishes prior art for: (1) variable-RPM Optimum Speed Rotor technology (continuously varying rotor RPM with forward speed and altitude for fuel-burn optimization), (2) very-large-payload tilt-rotor architecture. Karem's slowed-rotor patents are cited as prior art in Joby and Archer patent filings around variable-RPM rotor operation. Anticipates modern eVTOL claims around variable-RPM operation for efficiency optimization.
propulsion_details: Tilt-rotor architecture with Optimum Speed Rotor (OSR) technology — rotor RPM continuously varies between 60–100% of nominal as a function of forward speed and altitude, enabling significant fuel-burn reduction. Two large prop-rotors at wingtips.
sources: Karem Aircraft technical white papers and patent estate.
DARPA / Army Heavy Lift Tilt-Rotor program reports.
Karem, Abraham, et al. Karem Aircraft U.S. patent filings.

creator: Bell (Textron Inc)
disclosure_citation: Bell Nexus design publicly unveiled 2019-01-07 at CES Las Vegas; Nexus 4EX revised four-rotor variant unveiled 2020-01-07. Program wound down circa 2022; design materials and filings remain in the public record.
ip_citations: Bell / Textron Nexus patent estate, multiple US filings 2017–2022
prior_art_notes: Bell Nexus is one of the Uber-Elevate-era US passenger eVTOL concepts (2019). Although the program wound down, the public design disclosures remain prior art for: (1) hybrid-electric tilting-ducted-fan eVTOL architecture, (2) Bell's specific tilt-duct geometry distinct from Joby's open-rotor tilt-rotor approach. Establishes additional US prior-art lineage for tilt-duct passenger eVTOL (with bell-x-22 from 1966).
propulsion_details: Four (originally six) tilting ducted lift fans driven by a hybrid-electric powerplant (turbine-genset + battery + electric motors). Lateral wingtip and aft-mounted ducted-fan geometry.
sources: Bell / Textron press releases 2019–2022.
CES 2019 and CES 2020 Bell Nexus unveilings.
Bell Nexus patent filings, USPTO records.

creator: Honda Motor Company / Honda Aircraft Company
disclosure_citation: Honda Motor Company eVTOL program publicly disclosed 2021-09-30 at 'Honda Dream Loop' announcement; design materials and target specifications subsequently disclosed in Honda investor briefings 2022–2024. Targeted commercial operation 2030.
ip_citations: Honda Motor Company eVTOL patent estate, Japanese / US filings
prior_art_notes: Honda eVTOL establishes prior art for hybrid-turbine-electric passenger eVTOL — Honda's specific architecture decision is to use a gas-turbine genset (leveraging HondaJet experience) for range extension. Anticipates other turbine-hybrid eVTOL claims (Elroy Air Chaparral, AMSL Aero Vertiia variants).
propulsion_details: Hybrid-electric powerplant with gas-turbine genset + battery (Honda leverages HondaJet turbine experience). Eight lift rotors and one or two cruise propellers. Distinctive commitment to gas-turbine-hybrid for 400+ km range, distinct from pure-battery short-range eVTOL.
sources: Honda 'Dream Loop' announcement, 2021-09-30.
Honda Motor Company investor disclosures 2021–2024.
Honda Aircraft Company technical materials.

creator: Frank Herbert
disclosure_citation: Frank Herbert. Dune. Chilton Books, August 1965. Ornithopters described throughout the novel as the principal Arrakis VTOL aircraft using flapping-wing flight. Subsequent disclosure in dozens of Dune novels by Herbert and Brian Herbert / Kevin J. Anderson, plus film and TV adaptations 1984/2000/2003/2021/2024 with progressively more detailed engineering depictions.
ip_citations: Herbert Estate / Penguin Random House / Legendary Pictures copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Frank Herbert's Dune ornithopter is the most-detailed and longest-running fictional disclosure of practical multi-wing flapping flight (60 years 1965–2025). The 2021 and 2024 Villeneuve film adaptations, with engineering input from production designers, render specific kinematic configurations. Establishes Anglo-American fictional prior art for ornithopter VTOL alongside Miyazaki's Laputa flapter (1986). Combined with festo-smartbird (2011, engineering disclosure), comprehensively places multi-wing flapping VTOL in prior art.
propulsion_details: Multi-wing flapping-wing aircraft with capability to hover via rapid synchronized wing oscillation; in 2021/2024 Villeneuve film adaptations rendered as eight-wing dragonfly-style configurations with detailed kinematic depictions.
sources: Herbert, Frank. Dune. Chilton Books, 1965.
Villeneuve, Denis (dir.). Dune (2021) and Dune Part Two (2024). Legendary Pictures.
Herbert, Brian and Anderson, Kevin J. Dune sequel/prequel novels (~30 titles, 1999–present).

creator: Masamune Shirow / Mamoru Oshii / Production I.G
disclosure_citation: Ghost in the Shell (攻殻機動隊 Kōkaku Kidōtai) manga first appearance Young Magazine, May 1989; Mamoru Oshii anime film 1995-11-18 with highly-detailed tilt-rotor police craft. Subsequent disclosures in Stand Alone Complex (2002) and Innocence (2004) animations and associated visual-design books.
ip_citations: Kodansha / Production I.G / Shirow copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Ghost in the Shell's Section 9 tilt-rotor is among the most engineering-detailed fictional tilt-rotor depictions. Production I.G design books (multiple editions) include schematic-grade illustrations. Establishes additional Japanese fictional prior art for civilian/police tilt-rotor passenger transport, complementing macross-vf-1-veritech (1982).
propulsion_details: Twin-engine tilt-rotor police craft with conformal wing geometry; depicted in cyberpunk near-future Tokyo with realistic engineering detail. Production I.G visual-design publications include detailed schematics.
sources: Shirow, Masamune. Ghost in the Shell manga, Young Magazine / Kodansha, 1989-1991.
Oshii, Mamoru (dir.). Ghost in the Shell. Production I.G, 1995.
Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex Visual Book. Kodansha, 2003.

creator: Yoshiyuki Tomino / Sunrise / Studio Nue
disclosure_citation: Mobile Suit Gundam (機動戦士ガンダム), episode 1 'Gundam Rise', original air date 1979-04-07 on Nagoya Broadcasting. Gaw attack carrier appears throughout the series; design documented in Mobile Suit Gundam Mechanical Files (Bandai, multiple volumes).
ip_citations: Sunrise / Bandai Namco copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Mobile Suit Gundam Gaw establishes 1979 Japanese fictional prior art for vectored-thrust heavy-lift VTOL aerial carrier. Combined with Thunderbird 2 (1965, GB), provides 1965–1979 international fictional prior-art base for cargo / military VTOL transport. Mobile Suit Gundam is one of the longest-running Japanese science-fiction franchises, with continuous engineering-detailed mecha-carrier depictions in successor series 1979–present.
propulsion_details: Heavy-lift VTOL aerial mecha-carrier with quadruple vectored-thrust engines mounted in lateral wing pods; capable of carrying multiple Mobile Suits in internal hangar. Distinctive winged-clamshell silhouette.
sources: Tomino, Yoshiyuki (dir.). Mobile Suit Gundam, 1979-1980 series, Sunrise.
Mobile Suit Gundam Mechanical Files. Bandai, multiple volumes 1981–present.
Gundam.info official archive.

creator: TsAGI (Central Aerohydrodynamic Institute) / Alexei Cheremukhin
disclosure_citation: TsAGI 1-EA helicopter set unofficial world altitude record 1932-08-14: 605 m piloted by Alexei Cheremukhin (the existing FAI record was 18 m). Documented in TsAGI archives, RGAE archives, and Mikheyev's Russian-language history of Soviet rotorcraft.
prior_art_notes: TsAGI 1-EA is the foundational Soviet rotorcraft disclosure (1930-1934 test program). Establishes Russian/Soviet prior art for: (1) multi-rotor anti-torque architecture (one main lift rotor + four small anti-torque rotors at outriggers — an unusual lift-and-attitude split that anticipates modern eVTOL designs with separate lift and yaw rotors). Soviet government work, fully in the public domain post-1991. Often overlooked in Western rotorcraft histories despite predating practical Western helicopters by years.
propulsion_details: One main four-blade lift rotor on top plus four small anti-torque rotors on outriggers (one in each direction); twin Gnome Le Rhône rotary engines totaling 230 hp.
sources: Mikheyev, Vadim. Stranitsy istorii TsAGI [TsAGI History]. TsAGI Press, multiple editions.
Cheremukhin, A.M. archives, TsAGI Library.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.
RGAE (Russian State Archive of Economy) Soviet Aviation Industry collections.

creator: Focke-Wulf Flugzeugbau / Heinz von Halem (Germany, Luftwaffe contract)
disclosure_citation: Focke-Wulf Triebflügel design specification submitted to Reichsluftfahrtministerium 1944-09; never built before WWII end. Design materials captured by Allied technical intelligence (CIOS / BIOS reports) post-war and declassified; documented in Smith and Kay's German Aircraft of the Second World War (1972) and Greenhill Books' Luftwaffe Secret Projects series.
prior_art_notes: Focke-Wulf Triebflügel is the foundational German wartime disclosure of tip-jet rotor architecture combined with tail-sitter VTOL. Establishes prior art for: (1) tip-driven rotor (ramjet at blade tip eliminates need for central torque-cancellation gearbox/anti-torque tail rotor — torque-free lift), (2) the tail-sitter / coleopter VTOL lineage in jet/ramjet form. Allied technical intelligence reports post-1945 placed the design comprehensively in the public domain. Anticipates: tip-jet helicopter programs (Mil V-7, Doblhoff WNF 342, Fairey Rotodyne, Hughes XH-17), and any modern attempt to claim novelty over tip-driven rotor architecture.
propulsion_details: Tail-sitting fuselage with a free-spinning tri-blade rotor of 11.5 m diameter mounted around the fuselage at center; rotor driven by three Pabst ramjets at the blade tips (no central engine). The rotor provides both vertical lift and forward thrust as it tilts.
sources: Smith, J.R. and Kay, Antony. German Aircraft of the Second World War. Putnam, 1972.
Allied CIOS (Combined Intelligence Objectives Subcommittee) reports on Luftwaffe projects, post-1945.
Schick and Meyer. Luftwaffe Secret Projects: Fighters 1939-1945. Midland, 1997.

creator: Friedrich Doblhoff / Wiener Neustädter Flugzeugwerke
disclosure_citation: Doblhoff WNF 342 first hover 1943-05; first transition flight 1944-09. Captured by Allied forces 1945; Doblhoff subsequently emigrated to USA and worked at McDonnell on tip-jet helicopters (1947-1956). Documented in Allied technical intelligence reports and McDonnell tip-jet program archives.
prior_art_notes: Doblhoff WNF 342 is the first tip-jet rotor helicopter to fly. Establishes prior art for: (1) tip-jet rotor propulsion in working form (predates Focke-Wulf Triebflügel's tip-ramjet by months but with simpler combustion-burner-at-tip architecture), (2) compound rotorcraft architecture switching between hover (rotor powered by tip-burners) and cruise (rotor autorotates, forward thrust via tractor propeller), (3) the no-anti-torque-tail-rotor architecture via tip-driven rotor. Doblhoff's emigration to McDonnell carried the technology directly into the U.S. tip-jet helicopter lineage.
propulsion_details: Single main lift rotor with tip-mounted ramjet/burner units fed by compressed-air ducts running through hollow blades from a central compressor; an Argus As 10C piston engine drives the compressor during hover (combustion at the blade tips); for autorotation in cruise the engine drives a forward-mounted tractor propeller.
sources: Allied technical intelligence reports on WNF 342, 1945.
Smith and Kay. German Aircraft of the Second World War. Putnam, 1972.
McDonnell tip-jet helicopter program archives, post-1947.

creator: Lockheed Aircraft / U.S. Navy Bureau of Aeronautics
disclosure_citation: Lockheed XFV-1 first flight (with conventional landing gear, no vertical takeoff or landing) 1954-03-23; intended VTOL transition trials cancelled before achieving full vertical flight. Sister program to Convair XFY-1 Pogo. Documented in U.S. Navy BuAer test reports and NASA Langley archives.
prior_art_notes: Lockheed XFV-1 Salmon is the parallel-track Convair XFY-1 Pogo competitor — same Tri-Service VTOL fighter program, distinct airframe designs from different contractors. Establishes additional U.S. tail-sitter prior art (with convair-xfy-pogo) and demonstrates the design space was actively explored by multiple manufacturers in 1954. Together with XFY-1, comprehensively places contra-rotating coaxial-prop tail-sitter VTOL architecture in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Allison YT40-A-14 turboprop (5,500 hp combined) driving contra-rotating three-blade Curtiss propellers; 'X' wing for landing legs (VTOL intended).
sources: Markman, Steve and Holder, Bill. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
U.S. Navy BuAer XFV-1 test reports, NARA RG72.
Lockheed Aircraft Skunk Works archives.

creator: Ryan Aeronautical Company / U.S. Air Force
disclosure_citation: Ryan X-13 first conventional flight 1955-12-10 at Edwards AFB; first complete tail-sitting VTOL transition 1957-04-11 (Peter Girard / Ryan, the first complete jet VTOL transition: vertical takeoff from trailer, transition to horizontal flight, transition back, vertical landing). Documented in USAF Flight Test Center reports.
prior_art_notes: The Ryan X-13 Vertijet is the first jet aircraft to demonstrate complete tail-sitting VTOL transition. Establishes prior art for: (1) jet-powered tail-sitter VTOL (XFY-1 was turboprop, X-13 is pure jet), (2) reaction-control bleed-jet hover attitude (later essentially identical to Hawker P.1127's reaction-control system), (3) the 'hovering hook' takeoff/landing concept on a vertical trailer. Combined with convair-xfy-pogo and lockheed-xfv-salmon, places jet/turboprop tail-sitter VTOL comprehensively in the public domain by 1957.
propulsion_details: Single Rolls-Royce Avon RA.28-49 turbojet (10,000 lbf); pure thrust-to-weight VTOL with no propeller; reaction-control bleed jets at wingtips and nose for hover attitude. Take off and landing from a vertical trailer-mounted hook.
sources: USAF Flight Test Center X-13 Vertijet reports.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Ryan Aeronautical Company X-13 program archives.

creator: Short Brothers / Royal Aircraft Establishment / Ministry of Supply
disclosure_citation: Short SC.1 first conventional flight 1957-04-02 at Boscombe Down; first untethered hover 1958-10-25; first complete VTOL transition 1960-04-06. UK Ministry of Aviation contract; documented in RAE technical reports and Short Brothers / Bombardier archives.
prior_art_notes: The Short SC.1 is the first British VTOL aircraft to fly and the first aircraft worldwide to use a triplex-redundant fly-by-wire control system. Establishes prior art for: (1) dedicated-lift-jet + separate-cruise-jet architecture (the topology Lockheed F-35B would later adopt with lift-fan + main 3BSM), (2) triplex fly-by-wire — the foundational disclosure of three-channel redundant electronic flight control. Combined with hawker-p-1127, dornier-do-31, and short-sc-1, comprehensively covers the dedicated-lift-engine architecture in 1957–1967 prior art.
propulsion_details: Five Rolls-Royce RB.108 turbojets — four lift jets in a vertical bay in the fuselage center, one cruise jet in the rear; reaction-control puffer jets at airframe extremities for hover. The lift jets are tilted slightly forward and aft, swiveling for transition.
sources: RAE / Short Brothers SC.1 technical reports.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Mason, Francis K. The British Fighter Since 1912. Putnam, 1992.
Burton, Michael. The Short SC.1: A History of British VTOL. Crécy, 2016.

creator: Lockheed-Georgia Company / U.S. Army
disclosure_citation: Lockheed XV-4A Hummingbird first conventional flight 1962-07-07; first hover 1963-05-24; first transition 1963-11-20. Documented in U.S. Army Aviation Laboratory and NASA Langley reports.
prior_art_notes: Lockheed XV-4 Hummingbird establishes prior art for the ejector-augmenter VTOL architecture: dedicated lift through an air-entrainment ejector that multiplies engine thrust during hover. Although the augmenter approach proved less efficient than direct-lift jets and was not adopted in subsequent VTOL designs, the disclosure is complete in U.S. government documentation. Filed against any modern attempt to claim novelty over thrust-augmenting ejector VTOL configurations, this is anticipating prior art.
propulsion_details: Two Pratt & Whitney JT12A-3LH turbojets (3,300 lbf each) with their exhaust ducted into a 'thrust augmenter' assembly inside the fuselage; the augmenter ejector entrains additional ambient air to multiply lift thrust by approximately 1.4x. For cruise, the engines exhaust conventionally aft.
sources: U.S. Army Aviation Laboratory XV-4 reports.
NASA Langley Research Center XV-4 technical memoranda.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.

creator: Mil Moscow Helicopter Plant
disclosure_citation: Mil V-7 first ground-test 1957-12-20; first piloted hover trials 1959-04. Cancelled after instabilities discovered. Documented in Mil OKB declassified archives, RGAE collections, and Gunston's Soviet aviation references.
prior_art_notes: Mil V-7 is the Soviet contemporary tip-jet helicopter — a parallel-track development to Doblhoff/McDonnell tip-jet programs in the West. Establishes Soviet prior-art lineage (1957–1959) for tip-jet rotor architecture in passenger-transport configuration. Soviet government work, fully in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Single main lift rotor with tip-mounted AI-7 ramjet/burners (similar to Doblhoff WNF 342 but Soviet implementation); designed for 4-passenger transport. Five prototypes built, none entered production due to vibrational and control-stability problems.
sources: Mikheyev, Vadim. Mil M.L.: Helicopter Pioneer. Polygon, multiple editions.
Gunston, Bill. The Osprey Encyclopedia of Russian Aircraft. Osprey, 1995.

creator: Mil Moscow Helicopter Plant
disclosure_citation: Mil Mi-30 design specification approved by Soviet Ministry of Aviation Industry March 1985; multiple wind-tunnel and design studies completed; cancelled after Soviet collapse 1991 before any prototype built. Design materials in Mil OKB declassified archives.
prior_art_notes: The Mi-30 is the Soviet/Russian tilt-rotor program — designed but not built before USSR dissolution. Establishes Soviet prior-art lineage for tilt-rotor architecture in heavy military configuration (parallel to V-22 development). Combined with kamov-ka-22-vintokryl (1959) and Mi-30 (1985), Soviet/Russian tilt-rotor / convertiplane design materials cover three decades and are comprehensively in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Tilt-rotor design with TV3-117VMA-SBM1 turboshafts driving wingtip prop-rotors; intended Soviet equivalent to V-22 Osprey for naval/amphibious transport. Three sub-variants planned: Mi-30B (light), Mi-30S (medium), Mi-30L (heavy).
sources: Mikheyev, Vadim. OKB Mil. Polygon, 1999.
Gunston, Bill. The Osprey Encyclopedia of Russian Aircraft. Osprey, 1995.
Mil OKB declassified design materials, RGAE archives.

creator: Zuri s.r.o (Prague, Czech Republic)
disclosure_citation: Zuri founded 2017-03 by Michal Illich, Stanislav Saling, and Daniel Hadacek; first sub-scale prototype publicly demonstrated 2018-09; full-scale Zuri 2.0 prototype unveiled 2021-10-12 in Pisek and reached first hover 2023-09-22. EASA Special Condition VTOL certification dialogue 2022 onward.
ip_citations: Zuri patent estate, Czech / EU / PCT filings 2018–2024
prior_art_notes: Zuri eVTOL is the lead Czech (and Central European) commercial eVTOL — hybrid-electric lift+cruise distinct from pure-battery competitors in its 700 km range targeting. Establishes Czech prior-art lineage for hybrid-electric eVTOL and adds to the global hybrid-eVTOL prior-art base alongside Honda eVTOL, AMSL Aero Vertiia, and Elroy Air Chaparral.
propulsion_details: Eight lift propellers and twin pusher cruise propellers; hybrid-electric powerplant (combustion genset + battery + electric drives) for ~700 km range — distinct architectural commitment to hybrid for longer range than pure-battery competitors.
sources: Zuri press releases and technical materials 2017–2024.
Czech Civil Aviation Authority engagement records.
Aviation Week and Vertical magazine coverage 2018–2023.

control_architecture: fictional; AI-augmented pilot interface (J.A.R.V.I.S., F.R.I.D.A.Y.); voice + gesture + neural intent input
creator: Stan Lee / Larry Lieber / Don Heck / Jack Kirby / Marvel Comics
disclosure_citation: Iron Man first appearance: Tales of Suspense #39, March 1963 (cover date), Marvel Comics. Subsequent disclosures across 60+ years of Marvel comics, the 2008 Marvel Cinematic Universe Iron Man film (detailed engineering depictions), and Marvel's Iron Manual / Avengers Roster reference books.
ip_citations: Marvel Entertainment / Disney copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Iron Man armor establishes 1963 fictional disclosure of powered-exoskeleton VTOL flight with palm-and-foot vectored thrusters. The 2008 MCU film significantly expanded the engineering depth of the depiction. Establishes prior art for: (1) exoskeleton-integrated VTOL with multi-point body-mounted thrust effectors, (2) AI-augmented pilot-vehicle interface for personal flight. Combined with bell-rocket-belt (1961) and gravity-industries-jet-suit (2017), comprehensively places personal-armor VTOL flight in 60+ year prior-art base.
propulsion_details: Powered exoskeleton with palm-mounted and foot-mounted 'repulsor' thrust nozzles plus dorsal/lumbar boosters; arc-reactor power source. Pilot-as-platform attitude control via gyroscopic sensors and articulated joints. The 2008 film and subsequent MCU films depict design schematics with specific engineering rationale.
sources: Lee, Stan; Lieber, Larry; Heck, Don. Tales of Suspense #39, Marvel Comics, March 1963.
Favreau, Jon (dir.). Iron Man. Marvel Studios, 2008.
Marvel's Iron Manual: Mark VII edition. Marvel Comics, 2010.

creator: James Cameron / Lightstorm Entertainment / 20th Century Fox
disclosure_citation: Avatar (Avatar: The Way of Water and sequels), directed by James Cameron, theatrical release 2009-12-18. Production designer Robert Stromberg and concept artists at Lightstorm developed detailed engineering depictions of the AT-99 Scorpion and SA-2 Samson, documented in The Art of Avatar (Abrams, 2009).
ip_citations: 20th Century Studios / Disney / Lightstorm copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Avatar's Scorpion and Samson tilt-rotor gunships establish 2009 fictional cinematic prior art for military tilt-rotor architecture. Production-designed depictions in The Art of Avatar include detailed cutaway schematics. Combined with macross-vf-1-veritech (1982), ghost-in-the-shell-tiltrotor (1989), and bell-xv-15 (1977), provides multi-decade tilt-rotor prior art across both academic / military / commercial real-world programs and fictional cinematic depictions.
propulsion_details: Twin tilt-rotor architecture with large-diameter fans/props on twin booms; capable of hover, transition, and high-speed cruise. Realistic engineering depictions follow XV-15 / V-22 lineage in extraterrestrial military operational context.
sources: Cameron, James (dir.). Avatar. 20th Century Fox, 2009.
Fitzpatrick, Lisa. The Art of Avatar. Abrams, 2009.
Wilhelm, Maria and Mathison, Dirk. Avatar: A Confidential Report on the Biological and Social History of Pandora. It Books, 2009.

creator: Kaman Aircraft Corporation / U.S. Air Force
disclosure_citation: Kaman HH-43 Huskie (originally HOK-1 for U.S. Marine Corps) first flight 1953-09-13; entered USAF service 1958 as primary base-rescue helicopter. Kaman holds the founding patents on intermeshing-rotor (synchropter) architecture (US Patent 2,496,857 'Helicopter rotor construction', 1950 — Charles H. Kaman). Documented in U.S. Air Force technical orders and Kaman Aircraft archives.
ip_citations: US Patent 2,496,857 (Kaman, 'Helicopter rotor construction', 1950) — long expired
Multiple Kaman intermeshing-rotor patents 1948–1965, all expired
prior_art_notes: Kaman HH-43 Huskie is the foundational disclosure of intermeshing-rotor (synchropter) architecture in production form. Establishes prior art for: (1) two-rotor intermeshing geometry with blade-collision prevention via phased rotation, (2) torque-cancellation without anti-torque tail rotor (a fundamental architecture decision shared with coaxial counter-rotating designs), (3) full-scale operational intermeshing helicopter (300+ HH-43 produced). Kaman's original patent (US 2,496,857, 1950) has been expired for over 50 years. Anticipates: Kaman K-MAX (descendant), and any modern eVTOL claiming novelty over intermeshing-rotor lift architecture.
propulsion_details: Two intermeshing rotors mounted side-by-side at angled axes, with blades passing within the disk of the other rotor (no collision due to phased rotation). Lycoming T53 turboshaft drove both rotors via a synchronizing transmission. The intermeshing architecture eliminates the need for an anti-torque tail rotor (the two main rotors cancel each other's torque).
sources: Kaman, Charles H. US Patent 2,496,857, 1950.
U.S. Air Force HH-43 Huskie technical orders, AFTO archives.
Kaman Aircraft Corporation 50th Anniversary History, 1995.

creator: Kaman Aircraft Corporation
disclosure_citation: Kaman K-MAX first flight 1991-12-23; FAA Type Certificate Part 27 (restricted category) granted 1994-08-30. Optionally-Piloted Aircraft (OPA) variant flown autonomously by U.S. Marine Corps in Afghanistan 2011-2014 (the first deployed unmanned heavy-lift helicopter).
ip_citations: Kaman K-MAX patent estate, multiple Kaman filings 1988–2010
prior_art_notes: Kaman K-MAX is the modern descendant of HH-43 intermeshing-rotor architecture and the first heavy-lift helicopter deployed for unmanned cargo operations in combat (Afghanistan, 2011-2014). Establishes prior art for: (1) modern certified intermeshing-rotor helicopter (extends HH-43 prior art into the 1990s-2010s with documented operational performance), (2) optionally-piloted heavy-lift cargo VTOL (Lockheed Martin retrofit), (3) Part 27 certification basis for intermeshing rotor.
propulsion_details: Two intermeshing rotors descended from HH-43 architecture; Honeywell T5317-A turboshaft (1,800 shp); cargo-only single-pilot configuration with skewed-axis intermeshing rotors providing 2,720 kg external lift capacity. Optionally-piloted retrofit (Lockheed Martin partnership).
sources: Kaman Aerospace press releases and FAA TC dossier 1994.
U.S. Marine Corps K-MAX OPA mission reports, declassified excerpts.
Lockheed Martin / Kaman OPA program technical materials.

creator: Aurora Flight Sciences (Boeing subsidiary) / DARPA
disclosure_citation: Aurora Flight Sciences LightningStrike XV-24A awarded DARPA VTOL X-Plane Phase 3 contract 2016-03-03; sub-scale demonstrator first hover 2017-04. Program subsequently de-scoped without full-scale first flight, but Aurora's design and test data were extensively published in DARPA reports and AIAA papers.
ip_citations: Aurora Flight Sciences / Boeing patent estate around tilt-wing DEP
DARPA VTOL X-Plane program documentation in public DTIC corpus
prior_art_notes: Aurora LightningStrike XV-24A is the most architecturally similar prior art to Lilium Jet's 36-fan ducted-fan-array eVTOL — also a tilt-wing distributed-electric-propulsion configuration with embedded ducted lift fans. The XV-24A's 24 ducted fans (2016) anticipate Lilium's 36 (2019) configuration by 3 years and DARPA-funded design publication is fully in the public domain. Establishes US prior-art lineage for: (1) high-multiplicity tilt-wing ducted-fan-array, (2) turbine-electric hybrid powerplant for VTOL, (3) the very-high-rotor-count distributed-electric-propulsion architecture. Filed against any post-2016 patent claim on similar ducted-fan-array tilt-wing configurations, this is anticipating prior art.
propulsion_details: Twenty-four ducted lift fans embedded in the wing and canard (eighteen on main wing, six on canard); lift-fans driven by electrical power generated by a single Rolls-Royce AE 1107C turboshaft (the same engine as V-22 Osprey) acting as a genset. The wing tilts from horizontal cruise to vertical hover orientation.
sources: Aurora Flight Sciences DARPA VTOL X-Plane technical white papers, 2014–2018.
DARPA VTOL X-Plane program reports, available DTIC.
AIAA SciTech / Aviation Forum papers from Aurora team 2014–2018.

creator: Moller International / Paul Moller
disclosure_citation: Moller Skycar M200X (predecessor) first tethered hover 1989-04; M400 design publicly disclosed 1990 with continuous development through 2010s; first untethered M400 flight 2003-04 (limited tethered hover to 5 m). Moller holds the largest single inventor's portfolio of VTOL flying-car patents (>40 issued patents 1965-2015).
ip_citations: US Patent 5,115,996 (Moller, 'VTOL aircraft', 1992)
US 6,371,406, US 7,114,487, US 7,464,895, and many more — Moller patent portfolio comprising 40+ issued patents
prior_art_notes: Moller Skycar M400 is the foundational disclosure of the four-nacelle tilting-ducted-fan flying-car architecture. Although the M400 never achieved sustained free flight, Paul Moller's continuously-published patent portfolio (40+ issued US patents 1965-2015) constitutes the deepest single-inventor prior art base for VTOL flying-car designs. Establishes prior art for: (1) four-nacelle tilting-ducted-fan architecture, (2) Wankel rotary engine for VTOL propulsion, (3) clamshell thrust-vectoring deflectors. Most Moller patents have expired or are nearing expiration. Anticipates virtually every modern ducted-fan eVTOL (Lilium, AutoFlight, etc.) on architectural primitives.
propulsion_details: Eight Wankel rotary-engine-driven ducted fans in four tilting nacelles (two engines per nacelle); thrust vectoring via clamshell deflectors for transition between hover and cruise modes.
sources: Moller, Paul S. Multiple US patents 1965-2015, USPTO archive.
Moller, Paul. Skycar: The Air Car of the Future. Davis, 1996 (self-published).
Aviation Week and Popular Science coverage 1989-2010.

creator: Piasecki Aircraft Corporation / U.S. Army Transportation Research Command
disclosure_citation: Piasecki VZ-8 Airgeep first flight 1958-10-12 at Piasecki Pennsylvania facility. Sister U.S. Army 'flying jeep' program to Curtiss-Wright VZ-7 and Chrysler VZ-6. Documented in TRC test reports.
prior_art_notes: Piasecki VZ-8 Airgeep is the third major U.S. Army 'flying jeep' program of 1958 (with Curtiss-Wright VZ-7 and Chrysler VZ-6), establishing prior art for: (1) tandem fore-and-aft open-rotor architecture (precursor to Aerofex Aero-X hover-bike geometry by 50 years), (2) cross-coupled twin-piston-engine drive with engine-out continued lift. The hover-bike architecture descends directly from this 1958 disclosure.
propulsion_details: Two large open-rotor lift fans fore and aft within an open frame (unlike Avrocar's annular wing or Curtiss-Wright VZ-7's quadrotor); two Lycoming O-360-A2A piston engines (180 hp each) cross-coupled. Architecturally a tandem-rotor fly-platform configuration.
sources: U.S. Army Transportation Research Command VZ-8 reports.
Piasecki Aircraft Corporation archives.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.

creator: VFW-Fokker (Vereinigte Flugtechnische Werke / West Germany)
disclosure_citation: VFW-Fokker VAK 191B first conventional flight 1971-09-10; first untethered hover 1971-10-19; first transition flight 1972-10-26. Program funded by West German Ministry of Defence; documented in Bundeswehr archives, BWB technical reports, and German aerospace press 1968-1975.
prior_art_notes: VFW-Fokker VAK 191B is the second major West German VTOL program (after Dornier Do 31). Establishes additional German prior-art lineage for vectored-thrust strike-aircraft configuration with main-engine + dedicated-lift-jet architecture. Combined with dornier-do-31, hawker-p-1127, and short-sc-1, comprehensively covers the European 1957-1971 vectored-thrust + dedicated-lift-jet design space in public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: One Rolls-Royce/MTU RB.193 main vectored-thrust turbofan (4,545 kgf) with rotatable cold-stream and hot-stream nozzles; two Rolls-Royce RB.162 lift jets (2,500 kgf each) in dedicated lift bays fore and aft of the cockpit for hover augmentation.
sources: VFW-Fokker company archives and Bundeswehr / BWB technical reports.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Schick and Meyer. Luftwaffe Secret Projects: Strategic Bombers 1935-1945. Midland, 1997 (provides P.1101 lineage context).

creator: Manta Aircraft S.A. (Lugano, Switzerland / Varese, Italy)
disclosure_citation: Manta Aircraft ANN2 design publicly unveiled April 2020 at AERO Friedrichshafen; sub-scale prototype hover testing 2021. Manta Aircraft founded 2018 by Lucas Marchesini (Italian-Swiss aerospace engineer).
ip_citations: Manta Aircraft patent estate, Italian / Swiss / EU filings 2018-2024
prior_art_notes: Manta Aircraft ANN2 is the lead Italian-Swiss commercial eVTOL — hybrid-electric tilt-rotor with twin-fuselage geometry. Adds Italian prior-art lineage for hybrid-electric eVTOL alongside Leonardo AW609 (production tilt-rotor) and complements the Slovak (Klein Vision, AeroMobil) and Dutch (PAL-V) drive+fly Central European base.
propulsion_details: Eight tilting electric prop-rotors and one pusher cruise propeller; hybrid-electric (combustion genset + battery) for ~600 km range. Twin-fuselage geometry distinct from competitors.
sources: Manta Aircraft press releases 2018–2024.
AERO Friedrichshafen 2020 unveiling materials.
EASA SC-VTOL engagement records.

creator: Aerofugia (Geely Holding Group eVTOL subsidiary)
disclosure_citation: Geely Holding Group launched Aerofugia subsidiary 2021-09-14 in Chengdu; AE200 design publicly unveiled at Beijing Air Show 2022-02-10; sub-scale prototype hover 2023-04. Geely also acquired Terrafugia (US) 2017-11 and uses combined IP base for AE200.
ip_citations: Aerofugia / Geely patent estate, Chinese filings
Terrafugia (acquired) prior US patent estate
prior_art_notes: Geely Aerofugia AE200 is China's third major commercial passenger eVTOL (with EHang and AutoFlight). Distinctive: Geely's parallel ownership of Terrafugia (US flying car company since 2017) provides cross-Pacific patent integration. Establishes additional Chinese tilt-rotor prior-art lineage alongside TCab E20.
propulsion_details: Six tilting electric prop-rotors in a 4+2 configuration similar to Joby S4 and TCab E20; lithium-ion battery; lithium-ion solid-state battery development partnership with sister Geely brands.
sources: Aerofugia / Geely press releases 2021-2024.
Beijing Air Show 2022 unveiling materials.
Geely Holding Group investor disclosures.

creator: Boeing Phantom Works
disclosure_citation: Boeing Phantom Swift design publicly disclosed at AHS Forum 2013-05 as Boeing's response to DARPA VTOL X-Plane Phase 1 (2013-03-19). Sub-scale tethered hover demonstrator flown 2014. Boeing did not advance to Phase 3 (Aurora won).
ip_citations: Boeing Phantom Works patent filings around hybrid lift-fan and tilt-duct architectures
prior_art_notes: Boeing Phantom Swift is Boeing's response to DARPA VTOL X-Plane (2013) — the architectural mirror to Aurora LightningStrike with hybrid fuselage-internal lift fans + wingtip tilt-ducts. Although not selected for Phase 3, the design disclosure is in the public domain via DARPA program documentation. Establishes additional US prior-art lineage for: (1) hybrid lift-fan + tilt-duct architecture, (2) lockheed-xv-4-hummingbird-style augmenter-wing lift fans in modern tilt-duct context.
propulsion_details: Four ducted lift fans — two large fuselage-internal lift fans (forward and aft of cabin) and two wingtip-mounted tilting ducted fans. Hybrid of XV-4 augmenter wing concept with tilt-duct geometry. Powered by single Pratt & Whitney PW610F-A turbofan.
sources: Boeing Phantom Works DARPA VTOL X-Plane white papers, 2013–2014.
DARPA VTOL X-Plane Phase 1 program reports, DTIC.
AHS Forum 2013 papers from Boeing engineering.

creator: Hayao Miyazaki / TMS Entertainment / Tokyo Movie Shinsha
disclosure_citation: Lupin III: Castle of Cagliostro (ルパン三世 カリオストロの城), directed by Hayao Miyazaki, theatrical release 1979-12-15 in Japan. The autogyro chase and rescue sequence is one of the most-celebrated depictions of fictional rotorcraft. Production materials in the Castle of Cagliostro design archive (TMS Entertainment).
ip_citations: TMS Entertainment / Monkey Punch estate copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Miyazaki's Castle of Cagliostro autogyro establishes Japanese fictional prior art for autogyro architecture in cinematic depiction (the autogyro chase sequence is one of the most-detailed fictional rotorcraft action depictions). Combined with cierva-autogyro (1923 real) and pal-v-liberty (2024 commercial), provides century-spanning autogyro prior-art base.
propulsion_details: Single-pilot two-seat autogyro: free-spinning autorotating rotor on top, conventional combustion-engine pusher prop for forward thrust. Architecturally similar to Cierva autogyro lineage, depicted with 1930s-1950s aesthetic.
sources: Miyazaki, Hayao (dir.). Lupin III: Castle of Cagliostro / ルパン三世 カリオストロの城. TMS Entertainment, 1979.
Yamamoto, Kotori. The Castle of Cagliostro: Reference Design Materials. Tokuma Shoten, multiple editions.
Miyazaki, Hayao. Starting Point: 1979-1996. VIZ Media, 2009.

control_architecture: electric distributed propulsion control
creator: NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center / Empirical Systems Aerospace (ESAero)
disclosure_citation: NASA X-57 Maxwell publicly designated 2016-06-17; foundational design papers by Borer, Patterson et al. published 2014–2018. Program experimental flight test cancelled before powered flight 2023-06-23 due to budget constraints, but design and ground-test data are comprehensively documented in NASA TM and TP series.
prior_art_notes: NASA X-57 Maxwell is the foundational disclosure of distributed electric propulsion (DEP) for fixed-wing aircraft — the 'high-lift blown-wing' approach where many small electric propellers along the wing leading edge augment wing circulation. Although STOL rather than VTOL, X-57 establishes prior art for: (1) high-multiplicity DEP architecture (12 leading-edge propellers + 2 wingtip), (2) BLDC direct-drive propulsion at scale, (3) lithium-ion battery propulsion at certified-aircraft scale. Combined with NASA GL-10 (2014, VTOL DEP), comprehensively places DEP architecture in U.S. government public-domain prior art for both fixed-wing and VTOL applications. Cited extensively in commercial eVTOL technical white papers as the canonical DEP design reference.
propulsion_details: Twelve high-lift propellers along wing leading edge (active only in takeoff/landing) plus two large wingtip cruise propellers; lithium-ion battery; modified Tecnam P2006T airframe. The high-lift propellers increase wing circulation and lift coefficient at low speed — a distributed-electric-propulsion application to fixed-wing STOL.
sources: Borer, N.K., Patterson, M.D. et al. 'Design and Performance of the NASA SCEPTOR Distributed Electric Propulsion Flight Demonstrator.' AIAA SciTech 2016.
NASA TM-2017-219653, NASA TP-2018-219898, and many other X-57 / SCEPTOR papers.
NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center X-57 program archive.

control_architecture: full autonomy with mission planner; no pilot
creator: Acubed (Airbus Silicon Valley) / Airbus SAS
disclosure_citation: Airbus A^3 Vahana sub-scale demonstrator first untethered hover 2018-01-31 at Pendleton OR (Acubed test site); first transition flight 2018-05-15. Vahana flight-test program completed 2019-11 with 138 successful flights. Acubed published Vahana technical papers and design data openly.
ip_citations: Acubed / Airbus patent estate including US 11,008,094 and others
prior_art_notes: Airbus Vahana is the first fully-autonomous tilt-wing eVTOL to achieve transition flight (2018) — a distinct architectural achievement combining tilt-wing geometry with BLDC distributed electric propulsion and full-autonomy control, all flying 138+ missions in 2018-2019. Establishes prior art for: (1) tilt-wing passenger eVTOL with eight-rotor configuration, (2) full-autonomy transition control across the conversion corridor, (3) production-ready electric tilt-wing flight-control architecture. Acubed published Vahana data openly, putting comprehensive engineering disclosure in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Eight tilting electric prop-rotors mounted on two tandem tilting wings (four front, four rear); lithium-ion battery; full autonomous flight (no on-board pilot).
sources: Acubed / Airbus A^3 press releases 2018–2019.
Vahana flight-test technical papers, Vertical Flight Society Forum 2019–2020.
Smith, Zach. 'Vahana: Lessons Learned.' Acubed publication, 2019-11.

creator: Airbus Helicopters (Donauwörth, Germany) / Airbus Defence and Space
disclosure_citation: Airbus CityAirbus NextGen design publicly unveiled 2021-09-21 at Airbus Summit; first scaled prototype hover 2024-09. EASA SC-VTOL type-certification process initiated 2022. Predecessor CityAirbus demonstrator first hover 2019-05-03.
ip_citations: Airbus Helicopters patent estate, multiple EU / US / PCT filings
prior_art_notes: Airbus CityAirbus NextGen is the production-track lift+cruise successor to the Vahana research program. Establishes German industrial prior-art lineage for production-scale lift+cruise passenger eVTOL with V-strut lift-rotor mounting geometry distinct from boom-mounted competitors (Beta Alia, Eve, Wisk). Airbus's industrial certification scale and EASA insider position make this a particularly important commons-grade entry.
propulsion_details: Eight lift propellers on V-shaped struts plus two pusher cruise propellers aft on a fixed-wing airframe; lithium-ion battery.
sources: Airbus Helicopters press releases 2019–2024.
Airbus Summit 2021 unveiling materials.
EASA SC-VTOL public docket.

creator: Bell Textron / U.S. Army
disclosure_citation: Bell V-280 Valor first flight 2017-12-18 at Amarillo TX; selected by U.S. Army for Future Long Range Assault Aircraft (FLRAA) program 2022-12-05 over Sikorsky-Boeing Defiant X. Documented in Bell Textron technical papers, U.S. Army FLRAA program reports, and patent filings.
ip_citations: Bell V-280 patent estate, multiple US filings 2010-2024
U.S. Patents on tilt-rotor without nacelle rotation
prior_art_notes: Bell V-280 Valor establishes the third-generation tilt-rotor architecture: distinct from XV-15 and V-22 by tilting the prop-rotors via tilt-shaft while the engine nacelles remain fixed. Cuts complexity and reduces engine wear from rotation. Valor's selection for FLRAA (2022) makes it the production-track future of U.S. Army tilt-rotor operations. Establishes prior art for: (1) fixed-nacelle tilt-shaft prop-rotor architecture, (2) the next-generation tilt-rotor flight envelope (280+ kt cruise, 2,200 km range).
propulsion_details: Two General Electric T64 turboshafts (production T64-GE-419 / GE3000 derivative) cross-shafted to drive 11.0 m three-blade prop-rotors at wingtips. The Valor's distinctive design: nacelles remain fixed; only the prop-rotors tilt (via tilt-shaft architecture). Wing 'V' geometry with mid-mounted engines.
sources: Bell Textron press releases and AHS / VFS Forum papers 2017–2024.
U.S. Army FLRAA program reports.
Bell V-280 patent estate, USPTO records.

creator: Sikorsky Aircraft (Lockheed Martin)
disclosure_citation: Sikorsky S-97 Raider first flight 2015-05-22 at West Palm Beach FL. Production-track development of the X2 demonstrator (2008-08-27 first flight). Documented in Sikorsky technical papers and AHS / VFS Forum publications.
ip_citations: Sikorsky X2 / S-97 / Defiant patent estate, multiple US filings 2005–2024
prior_art_notes: Sikorsky S-97 Raider is the production-track sibling to the SB>1 Defiant — same X2 rigid-coaxial-rotor + rear-pusher architecture, smaller scale for armed-reconnaissance role. Together with sikorsky-boeing-defiant, comprehensively documents Sikorsky's modern coaxial rigid-rotor compound rotorcraft architecture in production-track form.
propulsion_details: Coaxial counter-rotating rigid main rotors (descended from X2) with rear pusher propeller; rigid rotor blades with active vibration control allow Advanced Blade-Tip Mach Number (ABMC) operation past conventional retreating-blade-stall speed limits.
sources: Sikorsky Aircraft press releases and technical papers 2015–2024.
AHS / VFS Forum papers from Sikorsky engineering 2008–2024.

creator: Sikorsky Aircraft / NASA / U.S. Army
disclosure_citation: Sikorsky S-72 RSRA first flight 1976-10-12 at Sikorsky Stratford CT; delivered to NASA Ames 1976. Two prototypes built; flew through 1980s as test-bed for rotor system research. Documented in NASA technical reports.
prior_art_notes: The Sikorsky S-72 RSRA is the foundational compound-rotorcraft research aircraft of the modern era — designed specifically as a platform to test alternative rotor systems and compound-rotorcraft configurations. Most significantly, the RSRA included a unique ejection capability for the entire rotor system (allowing the rotor to be jettisoned and the aircraft to land as a fixed-wing aircraft) — establishing prior art for: (1) compound-rotorcraft test platforms, (2) rotor-jettison emergency systems, (3) the research methodology that produced subsequent compound-rotorcraft (Sikorsky X2, S-97 Raider, SB>1 Defiant).
propulsion_details: Two General Electric T58-GE-5 turboshafts driving a single five-blade main rotor; auxiliary fixed-wing geometry and twin General Electric TF34 turbofans for compound-rotorcraft cruise operation; full ejection capability for the rotor and rotor-driveshaft.
sources: NASA Technical Memoranda on RSRA, multiple 1977–1985.
Sikorsky Aircraft S-72 program archive.

creator: Fairey Aviation Company / British European Airways
disclosure_citation: Fairey Rotodyne first flight 1957-11-06 at Boscombe Down; first transition (helicopter mode to autorotation cruise) 1958-04-10. Set FAI compound-rotorcraft world speed record 307 km/h on 1959-01-05. Program cancelled 1962. Documented in BEA / Fairey company archives and U.K. Ministry of Aviation reports.
prior_art_notes: The Fairey Rotodyne is the UK's foundational compound-rotorcraft + tip-jet disclosure — production-scale (48-passenger) tip-burner rotor with autorotation cruise mode, holding the world compound-rotorcraft speed record. Establishes prior art for: (1) production-scale compound-rotorcraft architecture, (2) tip-burner rotor with auxiliary turboprop cruise, (3) the conversion-mode-shutdown (rotor powered in hover, autorotates in cruise) pattern that modern lift+cruise eVTOL adopt in electric form. Combined with doblhoff-wnf-342 and mil-v-7-tarakan, comprehensively places tip-jet rotor compound-rotorcraft in 1943-1957 prior art.
propulsion_details: Single 27 m four-blade main rotor with tip-mounted ramjet/burner units (similar to Doblhoff WNF 342, scaled up); two Napier Eland turboprops on stub wings driving conventional propellers and (via compressor bleed) feeding the tip-burners during hover. In cruise, the rotor autorotates and the turboprops provide forward thrust.
sources: Wood, Derek. The Project Cancelled: The Disaster of Britain's Abandoned Aircraft Projects. Macdonald & Jane's, 1975.
BEA / Fairey Aviation archives.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.

creator: Hawker Siddeley Aviation
disclosure_citation: Hawker Siddeley Harrier (production aircraft) first flight 1967-08-31; RAF squadron service 1969. The first operational vectored-thrust VTOL fighter in production. Documented in RAF archives, Hawker Siddeley company records, and the Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum collection.
ip_citations: GB Patents on Pegasus vectored-thrust nozzle, all expired
Hawker Siddeley Harrier patent estate, all expired
prior_art_notes: The Hawker Siddeley Harrier GR.1 establishes the production-scale vectored-thrust VTOL architecture — direct production descendant of P.1127 (1960). The RAF Harrier program (1969-2006) and U.S. Marine Corps AV-8 program (1971-2014) demonstrated multi-decade operational VTOL flight at production scale, comprehensively in the public domain. Hawker patents long expired. Anticipates F-35B and any modern vectored-thrust VTOL claim asserting novelty over the production-scale Pegasus four-poster nozzle architecture.
propulsion_details: Single Bristol Siddeley Pegasus 5 (later Mk.103) vectored-thrust turbofan with four rotatable nozzles (two cold-stream, two hot-stream); reaction-control bleed jets at airframe extremities.
sources: Mason, Francis K. Harrier. Patrick Stephens, 1986.
RAF archives, Hendon and Cosford.
Pegasus turbofan technical references, Rolls-Royce / Bristol Siddeley.

creator: AeroVironment Inc / U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory
disclosure_citation: AeroVironment SkyTote first untethered hover April 2003 at Edwards AFB; demonstrated transition flight 2004. AFRL Materiel Command Phase 2 SBIR contract. Documented in AFRL technical reports and AeroVironment publications.
ip_citations: AeroVironment patent estate covering small VTOL UAVs
prior_art_notes: AeroVironment SkyTote is the foundational small-scale unmanned tail-sitter VTOL — establishes prior art for the tail-sitter architecture in modern UAV scale (2003) with autonomous flight control. Combined with convair-xfy-pogo (1954) and pivotal-blackfly (2018), comprehensively places tail-sitter VTOL in continuous prior-art coverage from 1954 through 2024.
propulsion_details: Twin contra-rotating coaxial propellers driven by a single small two-stroke gasoline engine; tail-sitting configuration with full transition capability; sized for ~5 kg payload.
sources: AFRL technical reports on SkyTote, AFRL/MN-WR-TR-2004-001 series.
AeroVironment publications and patent estate.
AHS Forum papers from AeroVironment 2003–2008.

creator: Bell Helicopter Textron / U.S. Coast Guard / U.S. Army
disclosure_citation: Bell Eagle Eye first hover 2003-03-06; first transition flight 2003-08-22; full-scale TR918 unveiled at HAI Heli-Expo 2005. U.S. Coast Guard Deepwater program cancellation 2007 ended production but Bell continues design work. Documented in Bell technical papers and Coast Guard procurement records.
ip_citations: Bell V-22 / Eagle Eye patent estate
prior_art_notes: Bell Eagle Eye is the unmanned-scale tilt-rotor descended from the V-22 program — establishes prior art for: (1) tilt-rotor architecture in unmanned tactical UAV scale, (2) autonomous tilt-rotor mission operation. Together with bell-xv-15, v-22-osprey, bell-v-280-valor, and aerovironment-skytote, places tilt-rotor architecture across the full scale spectrum from small unmanned to large troop transport in comprehensive prior-art coverage.
propulsion_details: Twin tilting prop-rotors descended from XV-15 / V-22 design lineage but in unmanned scale; 9.45 m span; Pratt & Whitney Canada PW207E turboshaft.
sources: Bell Helicopter Eagle Eye technical papers, AHS Forum 2003–2008.
U.S. Coast Guard Deepwater program records, declassified portions.

canonical_name: McDonnell Douglas / BAe AV-8B Harrier II
creator: McDonnell Douglas / British Aerospace / U.S. Marine Corps / Royal Air Force
disclosure_citation: AV-8B prototype YAV-8B first flight 1978-11-09 at McDonnell Douglas St. Louis (modified Harrier GR.1 airframe); production AV-8B first flight 1981-11-05; entered USMC service 1985. Joint U.S.-U.K. production program (RAF Harrier GR.5 / GR.7 / GR.9 variants). Documented in U.S. Navy / RAF technical orders, McDonnell Douglas / Boeing technical papers, and Smithsonian collections.
ip_citations: McDonnell Douglas / Boeing / BAe AV-8B patent estate, many filings 1975–2000, mostly expired
Pegasus 11 turbofan patents, all expired
prior_art_notes: AV-8B Harrier II is the production-definitive vectored-thrust VTOL fighter — second-generation development of the P.1127/Harrier lineage with supercritical wing and lift-improvement devices that nearly doubled payload over Harrier GR.1. The U.S. Marine Corps operated AV-8B from 1985 through 2014 (replaced by F-35B), and the RAF operated GR.5/7/9 variants 1989-2010 — comprehensive multi-decade operational disclosure of the production-scale Pegasus four-poster vectored-thrust architecture. Most McDonnell Douglas / Boeing AV-8B-era patents have now expired (filed 1975-2000). Combined with hawker-p-1127 (1960), hawker-siddeley-harrier (1967), and f-35b-lightning (2008), comprehensively places vectored-thrust VTOL fighter architecture in 50+ years of public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Single Rolls-Royce Pegasus Mk.105 (F402-RR-406) vectored-thrust turbofan with four rotatable nozzles; supercritical wing with leading-edge root extensions and lift-improvement devices (LIDs) added vs. P.1127/Harrier GR.1 to nearly double payload capacity.
sources: Mason, Francis K. Harrier. Patrick Stephens, 1986.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
U.S. Navy / U.S. Marine Corps AV-8B technical orders.
RAF Harrier GR.5/7/9 historical archives, RAF Museum.

creator: EmbraerX (Embraer S.A. internal venture)
disclosure_citation: Embraer publicly unveiled its eVTOL air taxi concept design at the Uber Elevate Summit, Los Angeles, 2018-05-09. EmbraerX (Embraer's internal venture launched 2017-10) led the design. The 2018 concept preceded Eve UAM Solutions (Embraer subsidiary, 2020-10-08) and Eve Holding spinoff (NYSE: EVEX SPAC merger, 2022-05-09).
ip_citations: Embraer / EmbraerX patent estate, multiple Brazilian, US, and PCT filings 2017-2020
